Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd get distracted or just not feel like it. Most weeks I'd cram things in whenever I remembered or when something was due soon. I spent around 25 hours supposedly studying, but a lot of that was just staring at materials without really absorbing anything. The anxiety made it hard to focus even when I sat down to work.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything academic, then panic when a deadline was close and try to catch up. Regular schedules never stuck for me because something always came up, or I just couldn't motivate myself to start. When exams or assignments loomed, I'd suddenly be online way more, but it wasn't consistent week to week.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or panic that got me to log in. Like I'd realize I hadn't checked the course in days and worry I'd missed something important. Sometimes notifications on my phone reminded me, or I'd see classmates talking about an assignment and think I should probably look at it. It wasn't really a habit, more like a reaction to feeling behind.
Q4: There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it routine, checking in every morning. But that fell apart pretty quickly when I started feeling overwhelmed and anxious about everything. After that, logging in felt more like a chore I avoided until I absolutely had to. It never became natural for me.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I'm a visual learner and I could pause and rewind when I zoned out. I watched a decent amount of video content, way more than I read the text materials. Discussion posts were okay too because seeing what other people thought sometimes helped me understand concepts I was struggling with.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and a lot of the supplementary resources. They felt dense and overwhelming, and I didn't have the energy to get through them. I'd open them sometimes and then close them after a few minutes. The optional practice quizzes I barely touched because they felt like extra work when I was already drowning.
Q7: If something felt like it would directly help me pass or understand something I was totally lost on, I'd engage with it. Anything that seemed abstract or not immediately useful, I'd skip. I needed to see a clear connection between the activity and not failing the course, otherwise my brain just couldn't justify spending energy on it.
Q8: Required activities had this weight to them that made me anxious but also eventually forced me to do something. Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford mentally. Like, why would I do extra work when I'm barely managing the basics? I think I did maybe one or two optional things the whole semester, and only because I was procrastinating on something required.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a bunch of slides and PDFs early on thinking I'd study them offline. I'd look at them on my phone during commutes or when I didn't have good internet at home since my resources are pretty limited. A lot of my studying happened with those downloaded files and some YouTube videos I found that explained things better than the course materials.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over my handwritten notes or rewatching videos I'd saved to my phone, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also study with a friend from class at the library, just talking through concepts, but that's invisible to the system. My LMS activity probably looked dead during those periods even though I was trying.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download the lecture slides or videos once, then never go back to the LMS version. It was easier to have everything on my device so I didn't need to rely on internet access. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I might have reviewed it multiple times offline over the following weeks.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart for me. My anxiety spiked, I wasn't sleeping well, and I started missing more classes. The workload felt impossible and I didn't know where to start, so I just... didn't. I had some personal stuff going on too that made it hard to care about school. That's when my attendance really dropped.
Q13: All of those things hit me hard. I was constantly tired and confused about what I was supposed to be doing. I had other responsibilities that ate into my study time, and when I did log into the LMS, I'd feel so overwhelmed by everything I'd missed that I'd just log out again. The fatigue made it impossible to concentrate even when I tried.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the LMS open, maybe a video playing, but I'd be completely checked out mentally. Just going through the motions without actually learning anything. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. It felt like I was doing something, but I wasn't actually there.
Q15: I avoided the bigger assignments constantly because they stressed me out so much. The anxiety would build up and I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, then tomorrow would come and I'd do the same thing. I knew they were important but facing them felt impossible. I'd do smaller, easier tasks instead to feel like I was being productive.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was breathing down my neck, then scramble to put something together. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and was dead otherwise. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I functioned.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially negative feedback, it made me want to engage less, not more. It confirmed what I already felt, that I was failing and not good enough. The CBT sessions helped me process some of that, but in the moment, bad grades just made me want to avoid the course entirely. Good feedback was rare but did motivate me briefly.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming. There was so much content released at once and I never felt like I could catch up. Weekly modules would have been easier than having everything available immediately. I needed more guidance on what to prioritize because I'd just freeze when faced with all the options.
Q19: The lack of clear structure made engagement harder for sure. Also, the assignments felt disconnected from the lectures sometimes, which confused me. What helped was when there were clear checklists or step-by-step instructions. Visual elements in the course materials worked well for me, but there weren't enough of those.
Q20: I think that was me sometimes. I'd be clicking around, watching videos, reading posts, trying to understand things, but when it came to actually producing work, I'd freeze up. The anxiety made it hard to commit anything to paper. Being active in the LMS doesn't mean you have the mental capacity to actually complete assignments.
Q21: I can see that happening if someone studies mostly offline or already knows the material. For me, low activity usually meant I was struggling, not that I had it figured out. But I know people who barely logged in and did fine because they used textbooks or had study groups. The LMS isn't the whole picture.
Q22: Logs can show when you're active and what you click on, but they can't show if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They miss offline studying, mental state, whether you're actually focused. They probably make me look more disengaged than I felt sometimes, and more engaged than I actually was other times.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking things, it's being mentally present and wanting to improve. I wasn't very engaged this semester, honestly. My head was somewhere else most of the time, even when I was technically doing course stuff.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I was dealing with or how hard I was actually trying despite my poor results. It wouldn't show the offline studying, the conversations with friends about course material, or the fact that I went to CBT sessions trying to get my head right. It also wouldn't show that I wanted to do better but just couldn't make it happen.
